scene_title: Rouen. A room in the King's palace

lines.speaker: KATHERINE
lines.text: Alice, tu as été en Angleterre, et tu parles bien le langage.

lines.speaker: ALICE
lines.text: Un peu, madame.

lines.speaker: KATHERINE
lines.text: Je te prie, m’enseignez; il faut que j’apprenne à parler. Comment appelez-vous

lines.speaker: KATHERINE
lines.text: la main en Anglois?

lines.speaker: ALICE
lines.text: La main? elle est appelée de hand.

lines.speaker: KATHERINE
lines.text: De hand. Et les doigts?

lines.speaker: ALICE
lines.text: Les doigts? ma foi, j’oublie les doigts; mais je me souviendrai. Les

lines.speaker: ALICE
lines.text: doigts? je pense qu’ils sont appelés de fingres; oui, de fingres.

lines.speaker: KATHERINE
lines.text: La main, de hand; les doigts, de fingres. Je pense que je

lines.speaker: KATHERINE
lines.text: suis le bon écolier; j’ai gagné deux mots d’Anglois vîtement. Comment appelez-vous

lines.speaker: KATHERINE
lines.text: les ongles?

lines.speaker: ALICE
lines.text: Les ongles? Nous les appelons de nails.

lines.speaker: KATHERINE
lines.text: De nails. Écoutez; dites-moi, si je parle bien: de hand, de fingres, et de nails.

lines.speaker: ALICE
lines.text: C’est bien dit, madame; il est fort bon Anglois.

lines.speaker: KATHERINE
lines.text: Dites-moi l’Anglois pour le bras.

lines.speaker: ALICE
lines.text: De arm, madame.

lines.speaker: KATHERINE
lines.text: Et le coude?

lines.speaker: ALICE
lines.text: De elbow.

lines.speaker: KATHERINE
lines.text: De elbow. Je m’en fais la répétition de tous les mots que

lines.speaker: KATHERINE
lines.text: vous m’avez appris dès à présent.

lines.speaker: ALICE
lines.text: Il est trop difficile, madame, comme je pense.

lines.speaker: KATHERINE
lines.text: Excusez-moi, Alice; écoutez: de hand, de fingres, de nails, de arm, de bilbow.

lines.speaker: ALICE
lines.text: De elbow, madame.

lines.speaker: KATHERINE
lines.text: O Seigneur Dieu, je m’en oublie! de elbow. Comment appelez-vous le col?

lines.speaker: ALICE
lines.text: De neck, madame.

lines.speaker: KATHERINE
lines.text: De nick. Et le menton?

lines.speaker: ALICE
lines.text: De chin.

lines.speaker: KATHERINE
lines.text: De sin. Le col, de nick; le menton, de sin.

lines.speaker: ALICE
lines.text: Oui. Sauf votre honneur, en vérité, vous prononcez les mots aussi droit

lines.speaker: ALICE
lines.text: que les natifs d’Angleterre.

lines.speaker: KATHERINE
lines.text: Je ne doute point d’apprendre, par la grâce de Dieu, et en peu de temps.

lines.speaker: ALICE
lines.text: N’avez-vous pas déjà oublié ce que je vous ai enseigné?

lines.speaker: KATHERINE
lines.text: Non, je réciterai à vous promptement: d’hand, de fingres, de mails—

lines.speaker: ALICE
lines.text: De nails, madame.

lines.speaker: KATHERINE
lines.text: De nails, de arm, de ilbow.

lines.speaker: ALICE
lines.text: Sauf votre honneur, de elbow.

lines.speaker: KATHERINE
lines.text: Ainsi dis-je; de elbow, de nick, et de sin. Comment appelez-vous le

lines.speaker: KATHERINE
lines.text: pied et la robe?

lines.speaker: ALICE
lines.text: De foot, madame; et de coun.

lines.speaker: KATHERINE
lines.text: De foot et de coun! O Seigneur Dieu! ce sont mots de

lines.speaker: KATHERINE
lines.text: son mauvais, corruptible, gros, et impudique, et non pour les dames d’honneur

lines.speaker: KATHERINE
lines.text: d’user: je ne voudrais prononcer ces mots devant les seigneurs de France

lines.speaker: KATHERINE
lines.text: pour tout le monde. Foh! le foot et le coun! Néanmoins, je

lines.speaker: KATHERINE
lines.text: réciterai une autre fois ma leçon ensemble: d’hand, de fingres, de nails,

lines.speaker: KATHERINE
lines.text: de arm, de elbow, de nick, de sin, de foot, de coun.

lines.speaker: ALICE
lines.text: Excellent, madame!

lines.speaker: KATHERINE
lines.text: C’est assez pour une fois: allons-nous à dîner.

glosses.phrase: Alice, tu as été en Angleterre
glosses.definition: French: 'Alice, you have been in England' — Katherine begins the scene asking about Alice's English
French: 'you speak the language well' — prompts Alice to give a lesson
French: 'A little, madam' — Alice's modest reply
French: 'teach me' — Katherine requests tuition
French: 'I must learn to speak' — signals the political motive: she is being readied for marriage to an English king

glosses.phrase: Comment appelez-vous la main en Anglois
glosses.definition: French: 'What do you call the hand in English?' — the lesson's first question
French: 'it is called hand' — Alice teaches 'hand' as the English word
Katherine's attempted pronunciation of English 'hand' — dialect-accurate; do not correct to 'the hand'
French: 'And the fingers?' — next body-part question
French: 'my faith' — mild oath, roughly 'goodness'
French: 'I forget' — Alice confesses a memory lapse
Katherine's attempted 'fingers' — French-accented English; a dialect trace
French: 'a good pupil' — Katherine congratulates herself

glosses.phrase: j’ai gagné deux mots d’Anglois vîtement
glosses.definition: French: 'I have earned two English words quickly' — playful pride
French: 'the nails' (fingernails) — next vocabulary item
Katherine's attempt at 'nails' — French-accented English
French: 'Listen' — Alice's instruction

glosses.phrase: C’est bien dit, madame; il est fort bon Anglois
glosses.definition: French: 'Well said, madam; it is very good English'
French: 'the arm' — next item
Katherine's attempt at 'arm' — the 'De' repeats her French-accented article habit
French: 'the elbow' — next item
Katherine's attempt at 'elbow'
French: 'I'm going to repeat' — Katherine recites to fix the lesson
French: 'It is too difficult' — Alice's gentle protest
French: 'Pardon me' — Katherine presses on
Katherine's mistake for 'elbow' — she has slipped; 'bilbow' = sword

glosses.phrase: O Seigneur Dieu, je m’en oublie
glosses.definition: French: 'O Lord God, I'm forgetting it' — her frustration
French: 'the neck'
Alice's English
Katherine's attempt at 'neck' — 'nick' is her approximation
French: 'the chin'
Alice's English for chin
Katherine's attempt at 'chin' — 'sin' is her approximation; builds the comic crescendo toward the indecent words
French: 'Saving your honour, truly' — Alice's courtly compliment
French: 'natives of England'
French: 'I don't doubt I'll learn'
French: 'by the grace of God'
French: 'Haven't you already forgotten' — Alice tests her
French: 'I'll recite to you promptly'
Katherine's slip — she means 'nails'
French: 'Saving your honour' — Alice's polite correction opener
French: 'So I say'
French: 'foot and the dress' — Katherine asks the English for these, but 'robe' is a mishearing; she probably means 'gown'
English 'foot' — sounds like French 'foutre' (obscenity)
Alice's attempt at 'gown' — sounds like French 'con' (obscenity). The scene's comic climax: English body-words become scandalous French
French: 'O Lord God' — Katherine's scandalized reaction

glosses.phrase: son mauvais, corruptible, gros, et impudique
glosses.definition: French: 'bad-sounding, corrupting, coarse, and immodest' — her list of objections
French: 'for ladies of honour to use' — what they should not say
French: 'the lords of France' — before whom she dare not speak these words
French: 'for anything in the world' — idiomatic 'for anything'
French: exclamation of disgust — 'ugh'
French: 'nevertheless' — she perseveres
French: 'my lesson all together'
French: 'Excellent, madam'
French: 'That's enough for one time'
French: 'let's go to dinner' — scene closes on a domestic cadence

annotations.title: Katherine's English lesson — the play's quietest political scene
annotations.body: Act 3.4 is an entire scene in French, broken only by Katherine's attempted English vocabulary words. It is a self-contained interlude: the princess of France, at her father's palace, systematically learning the body-words of the language of the man who is besieging her country. 'Le bras, le coude, le col, le menton, le pied' — the English-lesson is a domestic activity, but every word is a preparation for marriage. The scene works on two registers. The comic register is real: the accidental obscenities ('foot' and 'coun' — close to 'foutre' and 'con' in French) scandalize Katherine into an instruction-manual denial. The political register is that Katherine is being prepared for a conquest that has not yet happened but is already being arranged in the royal court. Shakespeare gives the scene no commentary, no choral frame, no English voice. It is pure preparation. When Henry finally woos her in 5.2, we will have already watched her assemble the vocabulary.
sources: Patricia Parker, Shakespeare from the Margins (1996)
Karen Newman, Fashioning Femininity and English Renaissance Drama (1991)

annotations.title: The deliberate obscene pun — 'foot' and 'coun'
annotations.body: The comic climax of the lesson is Katherine's shocked reaction to 'foot' (which sounds like the French 'foutre' — a crude word for sex) and 'coun' (Alice's attempt at English 'gown,' which sounds like the French 'con' — a crude word for female genitalia). Katherine's response is a full-scale courtly protest: these are 'mots de son mauvais, corruptible, gros, et impudique, et non pour les dames d'honneur d'user' — words of bad sound, corrupting, coarse, and immodest, not for ladies of honour to use. But she recites them anyway in her summary. The joke is that the English vocabulary of marriage-preparation is, from a French ear, a catalogue of obscenities. Shakespeare is making a very specific cross-linguistic observation about how sexual vocabulary hides in innocent words across languages. It is also a quiet comment on what marriage to the enemy king may bring.
sources: Margaret Jones-Davies, 'Shakespeare's French,' in Shakespeare and the Middle Ages (2009)
Juliet Dusinberre, Shakespeare and the Nature of Women (1975)

trials.prompt: What is unusual about 3.4 as a scene?
options: It is performed entirely in French (with Katherine's attempted English body-words), without English choral framing — an interlude preparing Katherine for a marriage Henry has not yet won
It is a sonnet, like the Prologue

trials.prompt: What is the source of Katherine's comic shock at 'foot' and 'coun' (lines 34–38)?
options: The English words 'foot' (close to French 'foutre') and 'coun' (close to French 'con') sound obscene to French ears — a cross-linguistic sexual pun
Alice has taught her the wrong words

trials.prompt: What political work does the scene quietly do?
options: None — it is comic relief only
It stages Katherine's preparation (vocabulary, by body-part) for a marriage to the conquering English king; when Henry woos her in 5.2, we have already watched her assemble the vocabulary
It introduces Alice as a new character for Act 4
It announces the French surrender at Agincourt
